crime: stealing 173lbs of Spanish wool, value 40, from the Essex, a lighter the property of Henry Salter, Henry Cooper and John Cooper, on 10 April 1805.
initial_sentence: death, commuted to transportation by 'the kind intersession of her ladyship the Countess of Cardigan'.
grounds_clemency: Hall is of previous good character and holds a position of trust, he has a wife and 5 infant children to support and a previous employer is willing to employ him again; Gray supports a wife and 2 children as well as his blind mother, has been recommended to Lady Hawkesbury by the Countess of Cardigan and he is willing to enter the navy (has been offered a place by George Anthony).
additional_info: John Hall, watchman, and William Gray and John McCarthy, convicted at the Old Bailey, London, May 1805, of stealing 173lbs of Spanish wool, value 40, from the Essex, a lighter the property of Henry Salter, Henry Cooper and John Cooper, on 10 April 1805. Evidences supplied by John Yerworth, foreman to Messrs. Salter and Coopers: Richard Jones, bookkeeper on board the Young John: John Gillman, Thames Police Officer: John Fox, Thames Police Constable: Thomas Taylor, lighterman and Henry Salter, lighterman. There is a letter from George Anthony, Lieutenant of 'His Majesty's Brig Deptford' offering to take Gray on board. Hall is of previous good character and holds a position of trust, he has a wife and 5 infant children to support and a previous employer is willing to employ him again; Gray supports a wife and 2 children as well as his blind mother, has been recommended to Lady Hawkesbury by the Countess of Cardigan and he is willing to enter the navy (has been offered a place by George Anthony). death, commuted to transportation by 'the kind intersession of her ladyship the Countess of Cardigan'. no further mercy. Folios 152-162.

crime: (fraud?)
initial_sentence: 3 years imprisonment and to stand in the pillory for 2 hours opposite the Royal Exchange.
grounds_clemency: none given.
additional_info: 1. 1 individual petition (prisoner) on behalf of Lawrence Jones, convicted (tried with Isaac Jesurun Alvarez and one Israel) on 16 April 1788, for a conspiracy to defraud Messers Wood and Co., at Leicester, of hosiery goods, value 156. The prisoners used the accidental fact of Isaac Jesurun Alvarez sharing his name with a 'gentleman' of Hackney in Middlesex, a man known to the commercial world and a wealthy man who had extremely good credit. The plan was to purchase goods from the various manufacturing counties on credit against his name, sell them on, and before repayment was required to abscond with the sale profits. There are letters from Mr Steele, Treasury Chambers; Mr Franklin, late Governor of New Jersey to George Rose, Secretary to the Treasury, Lord Cornwallis, General Sir William Howe and a memorial from Lawrence outlining his losses as a merchant during the American Revolution none given. 3 years imprisonment and to stand in the pillory for 2 hours opposite the Royal Exchange. 'Under these circumstances, I have known few cases less entitled to the Royal Mercy, or where an extension of it, would be attended with greater mischief, to the commercial interests of the country.

grounds_clemency: age (66 years) 'very infirm'.
additional_info: 5. Mary Wilson, for stealing a child 6 months old along with its apparel, convicted July 1791, 7 years transportation. age (66 years) 'very infirm'. Report of Brook Watson, Mayor of London on the 'Return of Women Convicts in Newgate, where ages or infirmities will not admit of their being Transported' Signed by F. Ramsden, surgeon. John Kirby, Newgate Keeper writes, 'There are none of the 23 Women here mentioned that are of bad behaviour, and not one of them, except Jane Wiggins (who has been convicted once before) were ever before in Custody.' as the prisoners cannot 'bear a Voyage to Botany Bay' and 'their Crimes of a nature not deserving further Punishment in Newgate..., could they be sent to the Penitentiary House at Clarkenwell, they would there be in a much less Afflicting situation'. If this is not possible then a free pardon to be granted and the women sent to their respective parishes. The report is annotated 'let all the Women named in the Enclosed List be pardoned to be passed to their several parishes, except Mary Wilson and Jane Wiggins alias Young who are to be pardoned on Condition of being confined for the remainder of the term of their sentences in the House of Correction Cold Bath fields.' Folios 192-195.

crime: stealing peppermint oil, value 60/8, property of James Moore and James Potter, 'physic gardeners' on the 13 September 1799.
grounds_clemency: he is innocent and did not know it was stolen, was 'never... suspected of any crime either dishonest or immoral', the victim will support mercy on condition of 'ample Security' being given, had lived locally for 4 years, he supported his aged parents and 2 orphaned children, has already served 9 months in prison, previous good character.
additional_info: (The prisoner tried with Isaac Martin, confectioner and maker of peppermint drops (guilty) and Jacob Dacosta, for receiving (not guilty)). There are chemists notes included within the notes of the trial such as 'an ounce of oil of peppermint would make a hundredweight of peppermint drops.' Evidence supplied by Timothy Jordan, 'chemist & druggist;' John Griffiths, an officer, Thomas Griffiths, an officer, John Nowlan, an officer, Samuel Samuels, Edward Harvey and Francis Moore. (James Potter did not appear as he 'is no more'[deceased]). There is a covering letter to the petition from James Moore, asking that he be given the security if Bergin is released. There is a covering letter to this from Francis Moore, cousin of James. There is another letter from James Moore stating his terms for the securities, 'himself (the prisoner) and 3 respectable sureties of 1,000 for future good behaviour, provided I have notice of the time appointed to accept such security in order that I may know his proposed sureties are responsible for the terms they enter into.'

John Kirby, Newgate Keeper writes, 'There are none of the 23 Women here mentioned that are of bad behaviour, and not one of them, except Jane Wiggins (who has been convicted once before) were ever before in Custody.' as the prisoners cannot 'bear a Voyage to Botany Bay' and 'their Crimes of a nature not deserving further Punishment in Newgate..., could they be sent to the Penitentiary House at Clarkenwell, they would there be in a much less Afflicting situation'. If this is not possible then a free pardon to be granted and the women sent to their respective parishes. The report is annotated 'let all the Women named in the Enclosed List be pardoned to be passed to their several parishes, except Mary Wilson and Jane Wiggins alias Young who are to be pardoned on Condition of being confined for the remainder of the term of their sentences in the House of Correction Cold Bath fields.' Folios 192-195.

crime: bigamy; marrying Martha Elder, spinster, on 7 December 1796 and Violet Bedford, widow, on 14th January 1799
grounds_clemency: innocent; he claimed to have been married three times and been tried for bigamy with wives two and three, however his first wife was alive when he married his second rendering that marriage invalid; his first wife dies before marrying his third wife, and so he is innocent of the charge, (the judge declares this as a legal but a 'most profligate defence'), prisoner did not have time to prepare for the trial, or obtain certificates, as he was only notified the night before of the time of his trial the next day, and he has 4 children to support.
additional_info: The conviction being on the marriage to Violet Bedford. Evidence supplied by Joseph Lee, parish clerk of St. Alban's, Wood Street, and George Avery, silversmith. The prisoner states that Jane Mayo was in fact his first wife, married on 2 September 1785. There is an extract from the Yeovil marriage register, signed by Thomas Tomkins, curate; an extract from the burial register of St. Saviour's Southwark, signed by D. Gilsin, curate, stating the burial of Jane, the wife of George Wills took place on the 20th May 1798; and an affidavit of Amelia Chiverton, stating that the couple were separated and the prisoner paid his wife maintenance (by deed). Jane (Mayo) Wills died on 12th May 1798. Martha left the prisoner shortly after the marriage because the prisoner met 'with some pecuniary difficulties and being arrested for Debt, she of her own accord deserted your Petitioner, and he did not see or hear of her for some considerable time afterwards.' The judge confirms in his report that had this information been supplied in an affidavit before the trial the trial would not have taken place as 'in strict law' there would not have been a case. Because the information has been supplied after the trial he takes a less lenient view.
doc_description: Report of John William Rose on  petition  on behalf of George Wills, leather dresser, convicted at the Old Bailey in July 1800[?] for bigamy; marrying Martha Elder, spinster, on 7 December 1796 and Violet Bedford, widow, on 14th January 1799

grounds_clemency: youth (22 years), previous good character and good education, first offence, always lived in credit, had bought the goods in good faith 'of a Jew' (he knows the man he bought it from but cannot get him while in prison - although he managed to get him initially committed), has an offer of employment, he was willing to return to his place of birth, the Island of St. Thomas in the West Indies, rather than be transported, prisoner has an aged mother 'considerably afflicted by [the prisoner] present situation'.
additional_info: Evidences supplied by the prisoner; Francis Hilton, Richard Tipper, city officer and William Banks, hatter. There is a statement of good conduct of the prisoner from John Airey, the prisoner's employer and another similar from John Parkin, grocer. The prisoner was taken up for bullock hunting ('a habit of dissipation') when the victim's hat was found on him. There are details of the prisoner's relatives, their occupations and the prisoner's education in the petition.

initial_sentence: 12 months in Newgate and a 5 fine.
grounds_clemency: previous good character, the prisoner needs to be pardoned before his 12 months imprisonment has expired to prevent disfranchisement as a liveryman of London, petition is signed by many respectable liverymen, charitable work has hastened his fall from 'Beneficial Trade' [bankruptcy] and the prisoner has paid his creditors 17/7 in the pound, he has the backing of many respectable people.
additional_info: Cole married Vaux on 24 June 1800 at St. Leonard's in Shoreditch, Middlesex, and Pilot at Hampstead Church, Middlesex. Cole had told Pilot that he was divorced. Pilot did not prosecute Cole, 'she was sought out by a Mr Page, one of the Singing Men in Saint Paul's'. Evidences supplied by Edward Gough, carpenter and father of Sally Pilot; George Lineam, Elizabeth Henderson, witness to the marriage of Cole and Vaux and sister to Vaux; Sally Pilot. Wood also petitions on behalf of John Smith, William Slade, George Biggs, George Lasemuth and Edward Kingsbury (details of military service and sentence given in the petition) [no longer present].

crime: murder, drowning his wife and child in a canal at Westbourne Green, [City of Westminster] on 5th December 1802 [Jane Foster, his wife, and Lousia Foster, his infant child]
grounds_clemency: Mrs Forster and the child fell in the canal and the prisoner was unable to assist them due to the deep water, there was some evidence that the dead woman may have committed suicide.
additional_info: The bodies of the wife and child were found at a later date which was considered to be proof of the guilt of the prisoner. If he had been there when they accidentally fell in they would have been recovered sooner; even if he were not able to save them. Evidences supplied by supplied by Eleanor Winter, Charles W----- [Charles Willd], William Garner and Joseph Broadfield. There is a letter from Mr Agutter, Asylum Chaplain, regarding a long consultation with Ann Arnold who was friendly with the dead woman. Arnold stated that Mrs Foster had parted from her husband and had gone into the workhouse. Mrs Foster and the child had since left the workhouse and were destitute. Arnold had told Mrs Foster to leave the child at the workhouse and obtain a nursing position, but she would not as the children 'were used so very ill.' Mrs Foster is stated to have said \"If we die we die together \\\"\"\\\"\" and that \\\"\"\\\"\"if something was not done for her she would put an End to her Misery.\\\"\"\\\"\" Eleanor Deker, who had met Mrs Foster at Arnolds, confirmed this statement and they both thought that 'Some mischief' would happen to Mrs Foster. \","Report of A MacDonald and John William Rose, Recorder of London, on George Forster/George Foster, convicted at [the Old bailey] of murder, drowning his wife and child in a canal at Westbourne Green, [City of Westminster] on 5th December 1802 [Jane Foster, his wife, and Lousia Foster, his infant child]

additional_info: The bill was received from Joseph Watkins, who purported to be an army officer and a fellow prisoner of war at 'Boudoux,' France. In the petitions there are details of the prisoner's childhood and education, his capture by the French and a note of ships he had served on. There are 2 covering letters to petitions from the prisoner in Newgate Prison. There are letters from the prisoner and one to the prisoner's mother from D.A. Hemsworth, Purser of HM. ship Formidable. There is a letter from H. Dundas to Mr Meheux instructing him to 'lose no time in calling upon Lord P.' and enclosing a petition from the prisoner, sent to Dundas' wife, who he claims kin with through the Stuart name. Lady Dundas' letter is annotated that there are very many favourable circumstances in his [the prisoner's] character, by William Baldwin. Lord Camleford's petition is annotated with a reply from J. King. There is a note from John Jackson requesting an audience with Lord Pelham on behalf of the prisoner.

grounds_clemency: the crime was committed during a drunken party, the crime could have been committed by the landlord who was of notorious character, it is unlikely that the prisoner would return to the scene of the crime once he had left, if the prisoner had kept the money he would not be starving in the prison now, was truly respectable up until this crime, the prisoner became insolvent due to losses sustained during the American War, the prisoner is of superior education 'to the generality of tradesmen', Forde considers the prisoner in danger of being murdered by fellow prisoners, first offence, the prisoner has been appointed clerk to the chapel in Newgate, the prisoner is too old to be transported (61 years) and too infirm for labour, blind in one eye, the prisoner has been of good character whilst confined and the prisoner has been awarded a double allowance of bread for his good behaviour.
additional_info: Evidences supplied by Peter Martin, wine and brandy merchant; John Barker, William Bell, iron founder; Henry Bell, high constable; Edward Mc Allister, keeper of the Castle Tavern; John Hall, William Coombe, William Nugent Cummins. crime: stealing 20 guineas and 4 doubloons, property of John Fields, of Chelsea
grounds_clemency: youth (29 years), first offence, has been in prison nearly 12 months, he is now in poor health and his confinement has almost cost him his life, the evidence was not clear and only circumstantial, is nave and was misused by the felons who since then were discharged upon giving sufficient amount of money to the prosecutor for his loss, he is reduced to misery and distress.
additional_info: The prisoner and an unknown person were an accomplice in an agreement to obtain money by selling a 'fake diamond ring' to the prosecutor, furthermore the prisoner did 'aid' and 'assist' the person unknown to obtain money by pretence from John Fields and deprive him of his property. The case was sent for opinion and 9 judges gave their verdict that the offence constituted 'felony' and not 'cheat'. This was in contrast to Lord Loughborough and Lord Chancellor Skynner's opinion. The written case as stated for the judges is also included.
